title: Denard Davis v. Director of Public Prosecutions
text:
COMMONWEALTH OF THE BAHAMAS
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL
SCCrApp No. 56 of 2024
B E T W E E N
DENARD DAVIS
Applicant
AND
DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
Respondent
BEFORE: The Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
The Honourable Mr. Justice Kokaram, JA
APPEARANCES: Mr. Denard Davis, Pro Se
Ms. Shaneka Carey, Counsel for the Respondent
DATES: 30 March 2026; 17 April 2026
*******************************************************
Criminal Appeal – Application to reopen a concluded appeal – Jurisdiction of the court to reopen
a concluded appeal – Exceptional circumstances – Residual jurisdiction - Real injustice - Material
irregularity in the course of the trial – Safety of the verdict - Interests of justice – Finality – Articles
28, 104 and 105 of the Constitution
In September 2016, the Applicant was tried and convicted of murder and attempted murder,
receiving sentences of thirty-five (35) and eighteen (18) years respectively. He appealed the
conviction and sentence, which was dismissed in August 2018. His subsequent application for
leave to appeal to the Privy Council was refused in November 2022. A subsequent application to
this Court (differently constituted) seeking to reopen his appeal was likewise dismissed.
In February 2024, the Applicant applied for constitutional relief, claiming his right to a fair trial
was breached due to incompetent representation by his privately retained counsel. His application
for constitutional redress was made over five years after his initial appeal was dismissed. His
subsequent appeal against that dismissal was dismissed on the 27 June 2024. He then made the
current application seeking to reopen that appeal. We heard that application and dismissed the
same with written reasons to follow.
Held: The application is dismissed.
In our view, there are no exceptional circumstances which warrant this Court exercising its
jurisdiction to reopen the Applicant’s appeal.

The Applicant has made use of his right of constitutional redress pursuant to Article 28 (1) of the
Constitution, as well as his right to an appeal to this Court pursuant to Article 104 (1). He still has
available to him the benefit of Article 104 (2).
We are mindful of the fact that as noted earlier, the Applicant has had his matter considered
previously by the Privy Council, who determined that, “there was no risk that a miscarriage of
justice had occurred in his case”. In our view, if the Applicant has any new evidence of substance
which could potentially change that position, he ought to avail himself of his Article 104 (2) right
which is still available to him.
Daniel Coakley v. R SCCrApp. No. 15 of 2017; applied.
Edney L. Burrows Jr v R; Thaddeus Williams Jr. v R SCCrApp. Nos 12 and 13 of 2021; applied.
R v Gohil; R v Preko [2018] EWCA Crim 140; considered.
R v Henry [2018] 5 LRC 546; considered.
REASONS FOR DECISION
Delivered by the Honourable Mr. Justice Evans, P:
Introduction
1. This is an application by the Applicant to reopen a concluded appeal. It should be noted,
for context, that the Applicant has had two appeals before this Court and the current
application relates to the second appeal. The matters, however, are interconnected and the
factual matrix relative to how those progressed are of significance.
Background Facts
2. On 3 November 2010, Sheria Curry and Shanko Smith were outside in their yard in the
Fox Hill area when a Hyundai SUV drove up and shots were fired into the yard hitting
them. Ms. Curry was killed and Mr. Smith was injured. Witnesses to the tragedy identified
the shooters, but no identification was made of the driver.
3. The evidence against the Applicant was contained in a statement he gave to the police
under caution. In his statement to the police, the Applicant admitted that he drove the
vehicle in the drive by shooting, but stated that he did not know what his co-accused were
planning. He was charged along with two (2) others for the murder and attempted murder.
4. The evidence from the eyewitnesses contradicted the evidence of the Applicant’s version
of events, and suggested that the Applicant, as the driver of the vehicle, reduced the speed
of the car to facilitate the shooting by the vehicle’s passengers. The evidence of the
eyewitnesses was that, the vehicle drove past slowly, and while driving slowly, the
2

passengers pulled down the windows and shot at the victims, then pulled off and stopped
again, and further shots were fired.
5. After trial, the Applicant was found guilty of both charges, and sentenced to thirty-five (35)
years on the murder and eighteen (18) years on the attempted murder. The sentences were
to run concurrently. The Applicant appealed his conviction and sentence. On 7 August
2018, the Court dismissed his appeal and affirmed the conviction and sentence. The
Applicant then sought leave to appeal to the Privy Council. On 9 November 2022, the Privy
Council refused leave, on the basis that there was no risk that a miscarriage of justice had
occurred in his case.
6. The Applicant then made an application for constitutional redress by a letter dated 5
February 2024 to the Supreme Court. It claimed that his Article 20 right to a fair trial was
infringed by the incompetent representation of his attorney at his trial, and at his appeal to
the Court of Appeal. He also claimed redress for breach of his Article 17 right, but that
claim was abandoned at the hearing before Hilton J (as he then was). After a hearing before
Hilton J on 21 February 2024, at which the Applicant appeared on his own behalf, Hilton
J dismissed the application with reasons to follow. Reasons were given in a written ruling
delivered on 27 March 2024.
7. The Applicant then appealed to this Court against the decision of Hilton J. In that appeal,
he alleged that he did not receive a fair trial under Article 20. He said that, the incompetence
of his counsel (whom he and his family privately retained and not one provided by the
Crown) produced an unfair trial, and that this issue raised a constitutional point on the
fairness of the trial. The act and omission of counsel he claimed, had an impact on the
fairness of the trial.
8. In particular, the Applicant said that his counsel was instructed to challenge the statement
and record of interview because he was beaten by the police to give the statement, and the
record of the interview. He alleged that a doctor’s report spoke to bruises and pain and
medication was prescribed. He further claimed that, notwithstanding this evidence, his
attorney did not carry out his instructions to challenge the admissibility of the statement
and record of interview on those grounds. That omission, he asserted, constituted a
miscarriage of justice, and that the failure deprived him of an acquittal, as there was no
other evidence establishing that he was the driver of the vehicle from which the shots
killing Curry and injuring Smith were fired.
9. That appeal (SCCrApp. No. 56 of 2024 dated the 27 June 2024) was dismissed. Subsequent
to that, the Applicant made an application to reopen his appeal against conviction which
was dismissed on the 26 November 2024. By the current application, the Applicant sought
to reopen the appeal relative to his constitutional motion. We heard the application on the
30 March 2026 and dismissed the same, and promised to provide the Applicant with written
reasons for our decision. This we do now.
3

Discussion
10. The legal principles relative to an application for the reopening of an appeal are pretty well
settled. In the case of Daniel Coakley v. R SCCrApp. No. 15 of 2017, Sir Michael Barnett,
P observed as follows:
“The jurisdiction of a court to reopen a concluded appeal in a
criminal matter has been the subject of consideration by this
Court in a number of recent decisions: see Omar Archer v
Commissioner of Police MCCrApp. No. 140 of 2017. Though it
should only be exercised in exceptional circumstances, it is not
disputed that the jurisdiction exists. I refer to two cases which
were referred to in the Omar Archer decision.
In R v Gohil; R v Preko [2018] EWCA Crim 140 the facts of the
case as extracted from the head note are that:
‘The defendants in two separate cases were convicted of,
or pleaded guilty to, offences of fraud and money
laundering. In the first case the Court of Appeal dismissed
the defendant’s renewed application for leave to appeal
against conviction. In the second case the Court of Appeal
dismissed the defendant’s appeal against conviction. Both
defendants later applied to reopen the final determinations
of the Court of Appeal on the grounds that the Court of
Appeal had been misled by the Crown’s failure to disclose
relevant material.’
The English Court of Appeal said:
‘110. On the footing upon which R v Yasain was decided,
namely that there is no difference between the jurisdiction
of the Civil Division and that of the CACD to reopen
previous final determinations, it can safely be said that of
the CACD will not reopen a final determination of any
appeal unless:
(i) It is necessary to do so in order to avoid real injustice;
(ii) The circumstances are exceptional and make it
appropriate to reopen the appeal; and
(iii) There is no alternative effective remedy.’
In the case of R v Henry [2018] 5 LRC 546, Henry was convicted
and sentenced for the offence of causing dangerous harm. He
appealed to the Court of Appeal which, by oral judgment eight
4

days following the hearing of the appeal, dismissed Henry’s
appeal and confirmed his conviction and sentence. At that time
the court indicated that its reasons would follow. When the
court’s written judgment was produced the court allowed the
appeal and quashed Henry’s conviction and sentence with no
reference to the earlier oral judgment which dismissed the
appeal. The DPP sought to appeal the written judgment on the
basis that the court became functus officio from the date of the
oral judgment.
The DPP’s appeal to the Caribbean Court of Justice (CCJ) was
allowed on the basis of errors by the Court of Appeal but in the
course of delivering its judgment the CCJ accepted that:
‘[17] …It is thus settled law that a court has an inherent
power to even reopen a criminal appeal to ensure that
justice is done...’
Of course, there can be no fixed circumstances where this
jurisdiction can be exercised, save that it must be only in
exceptional circumstances. This is particularly so in criminal
cases where finality plays an important role. This point was
made in Edney L. Burrows Jr v R; Thaddeus Williams Jr. v R
SCCrApp. Nos 12 and 13 of 2021 where the Court said:
‘22. The jurisprudence does make it clear that in criminal
matters, and the compelling need for finality, the exercise
of this jurisdiction must be restricted to exceptional
circumstances’.”
11. In our view, there are no exceptional circumstances which warrant this Court exercising its
jurisdiction to reopen the Applicant’s appeal. Article 28 of the Constitution provides that:
“28. (1) If any person alleges that any of the provisions of
Articles 16 to 27 (inclusive) of this Constitution has been, is
being or is likely to be contravened in relation to him then,
without prejudice to any other action with respect to the same
matter which is lawfully available, that person may apply to the
Supreme Court for redress.”
12. The Applicant has made use of his right to Article 28 (1) of the Constitution as well as his
right to an appeal to this Court pursuant to Article 104 (1). He still has available to him the
benefit of Article 104 (2). Article 104 provides as follows:
5

“104. (1)An appeal to the Court of Appeal shall lie as of right
from the final decisions of the Supreme Court given in exercise
of the jurisdiction conferred on the Supreme Court by Article
28 of this Constitution (which relates to the enforcement of
fundamental rights and freedoms).
(2) An appeal shall lie as of right to the Judicial Committee of
Her Majesty’s Privy Council or to such other court as may be
prescribed by Parliament under Article 105(3) of this
Constitution from any decision given by the Court of Appeal in
any such case.”
13. We are mindful of the fact that as noted earlier, the Applicant has had his matter considered
previously by the Privy Council, who determined that “there was no risk that a
miscarriage of justice had occurred in his case.” In our view, if the Applicant has any
new evidence of substance which could potentially change that position, he ought to avail
himself of his Article 104 (2) right which is still available to him. Parliament not having
prescribed any other Court pursuant to Article 105 (3), the Applicant is able to seek redress
from His Majesty’s Privy Council.
Disposition
14. It was for these reasons that we dismissed the Applicant’s application to reopen his appeal.
.
________________________________________
The Honorable Mr. Justice Evans, P
________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Smith, JA
________________________________________
The Honourable Mr. Justice Kokaram, JA
6
